remarks: Severity is high for juvenile PSMEM (~8-18 years), low for majority of mature and seedling boles, with some exceptional individuals. Swiss Needle Cast. Chlorosis and needle loss of 2-4th year growth, as well as ascomata observed on PSMEM throughout the site. Young stands (planted ~2000-2012) are most obviously affected, in some cases > 95% of individuals indicating and >50% retaining <2nd year growth. Rarely, mature trees observed with <2nd year growth.

ABBY_007 (mammal grid) is located >50% within the spray area and partially within a riparian buffer zone that was not harvested and likely not sprayed. The \'Havarti Sorts\' harvest unit was sprayed via helicopter on July 14, 2017. According to a posted sign, the chemicals were: \'Rodeo\' (EPA Reg. No. 62719-324), \'Chopper Gen 2\' (EPA Reg. No. 241-430), and \'Dust Extra\' (EPA Reg. No. 432-1552). The Havarti Sorts harvest unit was sprayed aerially on July 14, 2017.

Disturbed soil and vegetation along tick drag path. Soil sampling pit dug in incorrect plot by contractors. Vegetation also trampled/matted down in vicinity. Contractors mistakenly dug a soil pit at the ABBY_001 tick plot rather than the ABBY_001 base plot. This happened no earlier than 2017-07-17 and no later than 2017-07-19. NEON field technicians discovered the disturbance while sampling for ticks ABBY_001 for ticks on 2017-07-19. The soil pit location was along or adjacent to the tick drag path.

Posted sign identified the chemicals used as Garlon 3a (EPA Registration No. 62719-037) and Roundup Pro (EPA Registration No. 524-529). Posted signs announced herbicide application along state forest road L-1070 and spurs. Specified plots are located along this road and may have been sprayed.

Chemicals used not recorded by sampling crew. Mosquito sampling crew discovered that roadside herbicide application had occurred between September 7, 2017 morning and evening collections. ABBY_031 is located along the affected road and may have been sprayed.

remarks: The Heart Throb timber sale and associated road closures prevented access to several plots in the SE corner of our ABBY site from late-March through mid-June. While the sale was termed a 'variable retention harvest' across the management area, the plots listed above, notably distributed mammal plot 014, now have no standing trees within the plot boundary. The timber sale agreement states that the harvest method was estimated to be 90% ground based and 10% cable harvest. The dominant vegetation harvested was PSMEM, but ALRU2 and THPL are also mentioned in the timber sale contract. There are numerous slash piles scattered throughout the harvest area with sparse retention/return of POMU.

Had arborist cut down tree from tower guy wire. Tree was left on ground where it fell to decay naturally.

Two dead tree stems fell on the throughfall collector in BART SP1. We removed the stems and set them down on the ground next to the throughfall collector. This may have happened during a storm on May 4 2018. We visited the site on May 2 and there were no trees on the throughfall collector and we returned on May 14 and the trees were on the sensor. The throughfall collector was not level and the base was bent so we removed it until we get a replacement. Dead trees landed on sensor.

A tree had fallen across the BART soil plot 4, but was suspended over the sensors, not touching them. Arborists climbed trees and used rigging to gently lower the broken bole down to the snow on the ground and cut the stem into logs and moved them just out of the soil plot. There was 15-30 cm of snow on the ground and the arborists are certain that the tree didn't touch the CO2 sensor housings or other sensors, but since they couldn't see sensors below the snow they removed the stem from the plot so the tree wouldn't land on snow-buried sensors when the snow melts in the spring.

dateRemarks: These plots had infrequent visits during the period of logging. The site hosts hired loggers who had very little understanding of where our plots were located, so we do not have accurate dates of logging.
remarks: The logging targeted large dead oaks killed by the drought and gypsy moth outbreak of 2016, and the logs left on the ground are mostly the ~15 cm diameter branches left behind by the loggers. In some areas there were multiple layers of these hardwood logs. HARV_018_R: We estimated that the logging affected 63% of the 40m x 40m plot, and nearly the entire 20m x 20 m plot. Only logs and ferns (mostly Dennstaedtia punctilobula) remain in these areas. From what the plot looks like now, and what remains of the canopy, I would classify it as a deciduous forest. HARV_012_R : more than 50% of the plot was logged. The area is now covered by ferns and tree seedlings, but many of them are Pinus strobus seedlings. Since the oaks were targeted for removal, I believe the classification of HARV_012_R should change to mixed forest. About 30% of HARV_004_R was logged in late 2019-early 2020. The logged area is currently dominated by ferns, but it includes tree seedlings.

remarks: Possible first NEON documented occurrence at this specific plot.

Hurricane Idalia's eye came ashore in Florida's Big Bend region near Keaton Beach, Florida at 7:45 am on August 30, 2023 with sustained peak winds of 125 mph, as a hurricane category 3. At peak, the Hurricane was a category 4 hurricane. Outer bands as the eye was still in the Gulf of Mexico hit Disney Wilderness Preserve on Tuesday, august 29, afternoon and evening. The eye and strongest gusts of wind traveled north of Disney Wilderness Preserve. Highest projected gusts in Polk and Osceola county which is where the Preserve was located was around 40 mph and 2-3 inches of rain.

The eye of Hurricane Helene made landfall on Thursday September 26th at 11pm about 10 miles SW of Perry in Florida. The hurricane was a category 4 with sustained winds of 140 miles per hour and traveled up the state of Georgia along Interstate 75 to North Carolina. Tropical storm force winds extended 350 miles away from the center of Helene. The hurricane was in the 90th percentile for storm size. The storm had veered to the right of the Jones Center where mainly JERC received heavy rain. According to the JERC tower which is on site on September 26th the tower received 74.5mm of rain and September 27th received 54.4mm of rain. There was low wind damage observed on site. The Flint River and Ichawaynochaway River were very high.

dateRemarks: Observations of needle browning were first recorded on 20230112 during phenology bout.
remarks: Emerging needles were reported for some Pinus palustrus during the phenology bout on 20221229. During subsequent bouts on 20230112 and 20230118 the buds and young needles had begun to turn brown and die. An internet search showed that our trees resembled those infected by a fungal pathogen possibly 'Tip Blight' Diplodia sapinea (https://hgic.clemson.edu/factsheet/pine-diseases/). This disease has the potential to impact several TOS protocols including Canopy Foliage; Litter; Vegetation Structure; and Phenology. This event is being recorded as Other (instead of Population Spike-pathogen) until confirmation from the OSBS site-host can be obtained regarding the cause of the browning/die-back. It is also unknown at this time whether the event will spread across the site.

Hurricane Idalia's eye came ashore in Florida's Big Bend region near Keaton Beach, Florida at 7:45 am on August 30, 2023 with sustained peak winds of 125 mph, as a hurricane category 3. At peak, the Hurricane was a category 4 hurricane. Outer bands as the eye was still in the Gulf of Mexico hit Disney Wilderness Preserve on Tuesday, august 29, afternoon and evening. The eye and strongest gusts of wind traveled north of Disney Wilderness Preserve. Highest projected gusts in Polk and Osceola county which is where the Preserve was located was around 40 mph and 2-3 inches of rain.

remarks: A large, old longleaf pine in the 40x40 area of the plot caught on fire during controlled burn. The pine continued burning until it hollowed out and then fell and caught the surrounding area on fire. However, it was a densely vegetated area between wetlands and the fire did not carry very far. So some parts of plot burned very hot and some not at all in a very patchy fashion.

The eye of Hurricane Helene made landfall on Thursday September 26th at 11pm about 10 miles SW of Perry Florida. The hurricane was a category 4 with sustained winds of 140 miles per hour and traveled up the state of Georgia to North Carolina. Tropical storm force winds extended 350 miles away from the center of Helene. The hurricane was in the 90th percentile for storm size. According to the OSBS tower on site the site received gusts of wind up to 48 mph on Thursday night on September 26th into the morning of Friday September 27th . On September 26th the tower received 5.05mm of rain September 27th received 37.3mm of rain. September 28th received 10.2 of rain. Downed trees were reported on site. OSBS_062 phenocam plot was directly impacted by the hurricane. Phenocam tree tag 6553 was knocked down during the wind event and will need to be replaced.

Hurricane Milton reached a category 5 over the Gulf of Mexico as it approached Florida. Milton made landfall as a category 3 near Sarasota, Florida with sustained winds of 120 mph on Wednesday, October 9th, 2024. Milton reduced to a category 1 hurricane as it moved further inland. Hurricane spawned tornadoes across the state of Florida. Ordway Swisher Center was north of the eye of the storm. According to Newsweather, rainfall from the hurricane was 3-6 inches. Gusts of wind were 44 mph. Plant Debris observed across OSBS.

dateRemarks: Unsure when cloud seeding operation began
remarks: Cloud Seeding Operation taking place across Left Hand Canyon Area From site host : The cloud seeding project has already begun and they have done it a couple times. They focus on storms that are moving east to west (ie upslope) and the focus area is the Left Hand Canyon area which leads them directly to Wild Basin area of the park along with your Allenspark site and the Leiffer Property. They are hoping for a 1% - 10% or more increase in precipitation to support water volume. This could change some of the data you all collect in some ways. https://www.9news.com/article/weather/cloud-seeding-boulder-county/73-91bf9436-068b-4da4-867c-ab708c6b8bef

dateRemarks: Flooding and washouts occurred sometime after the 2020 Creek fire, and before vegetation grew back and stabilized the slopes in spring of 2022. Burned areas were still largely bare of vegetation in fall/winter of 2021.
remarks: Areas west and upslope of plot SOAP_002 R (including SOAP_002M and SOAP_006M were burned down to bare soil in the 2020 Creek fire. Vegetation did not regenerate and stabilize the slopes until spring of 2022. This lead to minor flash flooding in the ephemeral creek beds in 2021, and fast moving water deposited a layer of stream sediment in plot SOAP_002 R, primarily in subplots 21 and 39. As of 2022, vegetation is regrowing over the sediment in the flood channels.

The Blue Fire incident started on 6/30/2021 in the middle of Soaproot Saddle (SOAP) just east of the tower. Power was lost to the site so all instrumentation was down for some time. On 7/12/2021, while the site was still without power, we discovered a tree had fallen on the line PAR sensor in soil plot 5. Site power was restored around 7/22/2021. A section of the tree around the line PAR was cut and removed on 8/25/2021 in order to ensure the sensor was not damaged and to allow for necessary maintenance. The slice was placed nearby. This event may impact the Quantum Line Photosynthetically Active Radiation data products for this soil plot for the time period the tree was over the sensor, and potentially change the time series data going forward as the remaining trunk of the tree was left in place which may change the amount of light reaching the sensor compared to before.

Spring time flood water was reported to be nearly chest-deep on the outermost row of the bird grid. Deep flooding was reported on bird grid STEI_033. Report was made by contracted bird technicians so details are limited. Technician reported that springtime flooding created dangerous conditions on the grid and that water that was nearly chest-deep had to be waded to complete the grid. While there quantitative measures of this flooding are lacking, it seems to be more than typical for this time of year so it is being reported.

remarks: Primary timber harvest species were Acer saccharum and Acer rubrum. Logging was supposed to occur more extensively across the western part of TREE but was abandoned due to wet conditions. dateRemarks: Disturbance occurred sometime between 2021 and 2022 DIV bouts. 20210629-20220727.
remarks: An animal (likely black bear) dismantled part of a log and dug up approximately 20% of the quadrat, although most of the quadrat is affected in some way. This resulted in the loss of many plant species and changes in percentages for most other variables in nested subplot 40.3.1. This occurred sometime between the 2021 and 2022 DIV bouts, but likely during the 2022 growing season based on lack of regenerating plants in affected area.

locationID: TREE_042.basePlot.all, TREE_046.basePlot.all, TREE_049.basePlot.all, TREE_051.basePlot.all, TREE_052.basePlot.all, TREE_053.basePlot.all, TREE_054.basePlot.all, TREE_055.basePlot.all, TREE_056.basePlot.all, TREE_058.phenology.phe, AIRSHED
dateRemarks: Logging occurred sometime over the winter - exact window and duration of logging unknown
remarks: Acer rubrum and Acer saccharum both heavily harvested. Some Abies balsamea also harvested but many not removed from the site leaving abundant downed trees. Apparent attempts to reduce understory competition evidenced by leveled areas of Abies balsamea and many individuals of Ostrya virginiana that were cut and left. Logging quite extensive with large amounts of slash on the ground. A list of plots with more specific logging effects: TREE_042- Evidence of logging equipment (dug up ground + deep tracks) leading up to the plot; lots of slash; bigger maples taken; smaller trees left downed. TREE_046- Lots of slash; smaller trees toppled and left; bigger logged and removed. Evidence of logging equipment present but no logging road. TREE_049- Lots of slash; smaller logged trees left behind; bigger maples logged and removed. Quadrat at 41.4 has been nearly completely covered by a fallen maple crown. TREE_050- Wooded half has many crushed trees and slash. Open part (Christmas tree farm) looks unaffected. dateRemarks: Overnight frost
remarks: Frost damage not perceived as significant below the canopy but some canopy tree species e.g. Acer saccharum have noticeable damage to young leaves

dateRemarks: Tree fell sometime over the winter and was observed by field teams in April
remarks: The south beetle trap on UNDE plot 003 was inaccessible due to flooding and a large down tree right on top of the trap. This could potentially change/affect the beetle community in the near area.

dateRemarks: The latest start date is 2019-03-01.
remarks: entire site-heavy snows blanketed the Wind River site in February and persisted into April. Entire site covered in heavy snow, multiple bouts of mosquitoes and litter canceled.

dateRemarks: 6/28 estimated to be peak heat day
remarks: Record-breaking temperatures affected whole region. Some plants on site experienced sunburn damage and were potentially otherwise affected.

dateRemarks: Tree fell sometime over night between 20220105-20220106
remarks: One of the largest Douglas firs in the immediate vicinity of the WREF tower was knocked down by a neighboring Hemlock somtine over night of 1/5/22-1/6/22. Because of the significance in size of this tree, some sensors readings going forward might slightly different that before it fell due to its absence.
